1: Micah the Morasthite — Micah was from Moresheth (or Moresheth-gath, 1:14), a village in the Shephelah (Judean foothills) about 25 miles southwest of Jerusalem — a rural agricultural community, in contrast to his contemporary Isaiah who was urban and courtly. His ministry spans three Judean kings: Jotham (750-735 BC), Ahaz (735-715 BC), and Hezekiah (715-686 BC) — the period of Assyrian imperial expansion under Tiglath-pileser III, Shalmaneser V, and Sennacherib. The address is to both Samaria and Jerusalem: both capitals are indicted.
2: Hear, all you peoples — shim'u ammim kulam: the universal summons opening a covenant lawsuit (rib). Ve-tiqshev erets (listen, O earth) — the earth personified as a witness; cf. Deut 32:1 ('Listen, O heavens; give ear, O earth'). The divine witness formula — Adonai YHWH bakh le'ed (the Lord GOD will be a witness against you) — casts YHWH in the double role of prosecuting party and cosmic witness. Me-heikhal qodsho (from his holy temple) — the heavenly throne-room is the legal venue.
3: The LORD is coming out of his place — ki hinneh YHWH yotse mimmekomo: the divine departure (yatsa') from his dwelling is theophanic language; YHWH does not leave heaven lightly. Yarad ve-darakh al-bamotei arets (he will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth) — bamot (high places) are both cultic hilltop shrines and the literal highlands of Canaan; the divine tread on the high places is at once a demolition of idolatrous shrines and a theophanic stride across the landscape.
4: The mountains will melt under him — ve-nimesu heharim tachtav: the standard language of theophanic poetry (Ps 97:5; Nah 1:5). The two similes: ke-donag mipnei ha-esh (like wax before the fire) and ke-mayim muggarim bemored (like waters rushing down a steep slope) — wax melts under direct heat; water rushes irreversibly downhill. The irreversibility of each image suggests the divine theophany cannot be stopped once initiated.
5: Because of the transgression of Jacob — be-pesha' Ya'aqov kol-zot: pasha' (transgression, rebellion against authority) is the leading accusation term in Micah. The theological equation that follows is sharp: Jacob's transgression = Samaria (not just located in Samaria, but is Samaria — the capital as the embodiment of national sin); Judah's high places = Jerusalem. The capitals are not incidental locations but the concentrated expression of the nation's covenant failure.
6: I will make Samaria a ruin heap in the open country — ve-samtiv le-'iy hasade: 'iy hasade (ruin heap in an open field) — the word 'iy can mean heap of rubble or pile of stones; the phrase denotes a city demolished to open farmland. Le-mataa' karem (a planting ground for vineyards) — agricultural de-urbanization; where a capital once stood will be vineyard terraces. Ve-higgarti la-gai avaneha (I will pour her stones down into the valley) — the stones of Samaria's buildings tumbled down the steep hill on which the city stood.
7: All her carved images shall be shattered — ve-khol-pesileyha yukkatu: the three elements of Samaria's cultic apparatus: pesilim (carved idols), ethnah (prostitution wages/fees — the sacred prostitution system), and 'atsabbim (idols). The key is ethnah — the term for the fee paid to a temple prostitute, also used in Deut 23:18 ('you shall not bring a prostitute's wage into the house of the LORD'). The accusation: Israel's idolatry was financed by sacred prostitution; the wealth gathered through fertility-cult prostitution returns to the same system — the cycle is self-perpetuating and self-destroying.
8: I will wail and lament; I will go stripped and naked — Micah describes his own mourning performance. Sholal ve-'arom (stripped and naked) — removing outer garments as a sign of extreme mourning or solidarity with those going into exile; Isaiah performed a similar prophetic action (Isa 20:2-3). Ke-tannim (like jackals) and ke-venot ya'anah (like ostriches) — both animals were associated with wilderness lamentation sounds; jackals howled at night; the ostrich's cry was associated with desolation (Job 30:29; Isa 34:13).
9: Her wound is incurable — ki anushab makoteiha: anush (incurable, desperate, grievous) is the same root as the name Enosh; used for wounds beyond remedy (Jer 15:18; 17:16). The wound has spread: ki va'ah ad-Yehudah (it has come to Judah) and naga' ad-sha'ar ammi (reached even to the gate of my people) — Samaria's disease is not contained at the border; it has infected Judah and is at Jerusalem's gate. The wound-and-infection metaphor anticipates Sennacherib's 701 BC campaign.
10: Declare it not in Gath; weep not at all — be-Gat al-taggidu: the opening of the city-lament series (vv10-15) echoes David's lament over Saul: 'Tell it not in Gath' (2 Sam 1:20). Micah adapts the Davidic lament for the Assyrian catastrophe. What follows is a sophisticated chain of wordplays (paronomasia) on Shephelah town names: each town's name resonates with its designated fate, a rhetorical technique that makes the judgment memorable by linking geography to destiny. Be-Bet le-'Aphrah hitpallashi afar (in Beth-le-aphrah roll in the dust) — Aphrah echoes afar (dust); a town named for dust will indeed be in the dust.
11: Pass on in nakedness and shame, inhabitants of Shaphir — the wordplays continue. Shaphir (beauty) — the beautiful town goes naked and shameful; beauty inverted. Lo yatsa'ah yoshevet Tsa'anan (the inhabitants of Zaanan do not come out) — Tsa'anan echoes yatsa' (go out); those in the 'going-out' town cannot go out. Mispad Bet ha-'Etsel yiqqach mikkem 'emdat (Beth-ezel mourns — it can no longer support you) — Etsel means 'beside, nearby'; the 'nearby' town is no longer near/helpful.
12: The inhabitants of Maroth wait anxiously for good news — ki chalah le-tov yoshevet Marot: Marot echoes mar (bitter) or mara (bitterness); the bitter-named town waits for tov (good) and receives disaster instead. Ki-yarad ra' me'et YHWH le-sha'ar Yerushalam (disaster has come down from the LORD to the gate of Jerusalem) — the disaster (ra') comes not from Assyria but from YHWH himself; the Assyrian is merely the instrument.
13: Harness swift steeds to the chariot, O inhabitants of Lachish — Lakhish echoes larekesh (swift steeds/chariot horses). Lachish was the second-largest city in Judah and a major military fortress; its mention here is historically precise — Sennacherib's Lachish reliefs (now in the British Museum) depict the Assyrian siege of 701 BC in detail. Reshit chat'at hi le-vat Tsiyon (you were the beginning of sin for the daughter of Zion) — Lachish is accused of being the entry point for Israel's syncretism (possibly because of its strategic position on the trade routes from Philistia).
14: Give parting gifts to Moresheth-gath — shillukhim Moresheth-Gat: shillukhim (parting gifts, the gift given when a bride leaves her father's house; cf. 1 Kgs 9:16) — Moresheth-gath, Micah's own hometown, is being 'given away' to Assyria like a bride leaving home. Batei Akhziv le-akhzav (the houses of Achzib shall be a deception) — Achzib echoes akhzav (deception, dried-up stream — a wadi that promises water but runs dry in summer); the deceptive town will not fulfill the hope placed in it.
15: I will again bring a conqueror against you, inhabitants of Mareshah — Mareshah echoes yoresh (possessor, conqueror); the town whose name means 'possessor' will have a new possessor brought against it. Ad-Adullam yavo' kevod Yisra'el (the glory of Israel shall come to Adullam) — Adullam, the cave where David hid from Saul (1 Sam 22:1-2), becomes the refuge again; Israel's glory has retreated from the exposed cities to the wilderness cave.
16: Shave your head and make yourself bald for your precious children — qarchah ve-gazzi 'al-benei ta'anugayikh: head-shaving was a mourning rite for the dead (Deut 14:1; Job 1:20; Amos 8:10); done for 'precious children' going into exile, the living are mourned as if dead. Harchivi qorchateikh ka-nesher (make your baldness as wide as the eagle's) — the eagle (or possibly vulture, nesher) was known for its bald head; the baldness of mourning should match the eagle's natural baldness. Ki galu mimmeikh (for they have gone from you into exile) — the exile is spoken of as already accomplished.
